title: Developmental Uncertainty: How Nascent Technology Shapes Skill-Building Through Work
data_source: JointBot: 2.44M teleop machine actions, 754 daily scan observations, 145 interviews, operator skill trajectories

events.version_name: Engineering Skill in HRI: Initial Exploration

categories.framing: How operators develop skill in human-robot interaction settings
categories.mechanism: Skill development through direct engagement with nascent technology
categories.evidence: 2.44M teleop machine action records, 145 interviews, 3 phases identified (Phase 1: 1.47 actions/item, Phase 2: 0.69, Phase 3: 0.12)
categories.claims: Three distinct phases of human-robot interaction skill development
categories.architecture: Standard mixed-methods: quant phases + qual mechanism

events.summary: Initial exploration of HRI skill data
events.detail: Applied theory-forge pipeline to dormant JointBot data. Found clear three-phase pattern in telemetry: operators go from high-intervention (1.47 actions/item) to near-autonomous (0.12 actions/item). Qualitative interviews reveal this maps to fundamentally different work roles.
events.trigger: Theory-forge pipeline applied to dormant data from Vicarious/JointBot study

categories.validation: ZIP CRITIQUE: Deep technical review of phase transitions and telemetry. Verdict: plausible ASQ paper, strong OrgSci candidate.

events.detail: External review (23.1KB) verified phase transition data: Phase 1 (1.47), Phase 2 (0.69), Phase 3 (0.12) are correct and reproducible. Key critiques: (1) phases are partly interpretive not purely algorithmic, (2) mobility numbers (76%, 19%) need explicit N, (3) 'developmental uncertainty' concept under-specified. Verdict: plausible ASQ paper.

categories.validation: Zuckerman evaluation: 9/10 after revisions. Perfect one-idea focus on 'developmental uncertainty'.

events.detail: Zuckerman criteria evaluation scored 9/10. Strengths: perfect one-idea focus ('developmental uncertainty'), strong quantitative anchors, excellent null-saving. Remaining: sharpen section 2.1 title, consider leading abstract with scope condition.

categories.claims: CLM-003 challenged: Later worker U17 advanced to supervisor, contradicting 'negligible advancement' claim. CLM-012, CLM-013: No supporting evidence found for engineer-driver collaboration.
categories.validation: Adversarial audit of 69 transcripts (15 in-depth). Mixed findings: CLM-001, CLM-004, CLM-005 supported. CLM-003 challenged. CLM-012, CLM-013 no evidence.
categories.replication: Living Paper infrastructure: 33 claims, 91 evidence links, verification checklist

events.detail: Adversarial audit of 69 interview transcripts. SUPPORTED: early technical skills (CLM-001), uncertainty mechanism (CLM-004), necessity-driven learning (CLM-005). CHALLENGED: CLM-003 ('negligible advancement') contradicted by worker U17 who advanced to supervisor. NO EVIDENCE: CLM-012, CLM-013 (engineer-driver collaboration central to mechanism). Created Living Paper infrastructure with 33 claims and 91 links.
Commit 164da3e: 'Initial commit: warehouse automation papers (manuscripts only)'. Project formally tracked in git.

categories.framing: REFRAMED: 'Inclusive Automation Development' → 'Developmental Uncertainty'. Shifted from describing what happened to theorizing WHY nascent technology creates unique skill-building conditions.
categories.mechanism: Developmental uncertainty as scope condition for contingency theory: when technology itself is uncertain, work design must accommodate simultaneous skill-building and production.
categories.claims: NEW: Developmental uncertainty as fourth mode alongside task, environmental, and relational uncertainty
categories.boundaries: Scope sharpened: applies when technology is genuinely nascent (not mature automation rollout)

events.summary: Major reframe to developmental uncertainty concept
events.detail: Reframed paper from descriptive account of inclusive automation to theoretical contribution about developmental uncertainty. Key insight: when the technology itself is developing, the nature of the work and the skills required are fundamentally uncertain — creating conditions where inclusive, cross-status skill-building emerges as a functional necessity rather than a design choice.
events.trigger: External review feedback on under-specified scope condition; theory-forge evaluation

categories.replication: Overleaf zip archived, cover letter finalized

events.detail: Paper submitted to Organization Science as 'Developmental Uncertainty'. Full submission package: main.tex, references.bib, cover letter, figures.
